results.title: Reliability Test Design
results.description: A reliability engineer wants to design a zero-failure demonstration test in order to demonstrate a reliability of 80% at a 90% confidence level. Use the non-parametric binomial method to determine the required sample size.
results.content:
A reliability engineer wants to design a zero-failure demonstration test in order to demonstrate a reliability of 80% at a 90% confidence level. Use the non-parametric binomial method to determine the required sample size.
In this case, one knows beforehand the number of units, , the number of allowable failures, , and the confidence level, . With this information, the next step involves solving the binomial equation for . With this value known, one can use the appropriate reliability equation to back out the value of , since , and , and have already been calculated or specified. In this example, we will use the parametric binomial method to design a test to demonstrate a reliability of 90% at hours with a 95% confidence if no failure occur during the test.
If those 11 samples are run for the required demonstration time and no failures are observed, then a reliability of 80% with a 90% confidence level has been demonstrated. If the reliability of the system is less than or equal to 80%, the chance of passing this test is 1-CL = 0.1, which is the Type II error. Therefore, the non-parametric binomial equation determines the sample size by controlling for the Type II error.
In this example, we will use the exponential chi-squared method to design a test that will demonstrate a reliability of 85% at hours with a 90% confidence (or ) if no more than 2 failures occur during the test ().
In this example, we will use the parametric binomial method to design a test that will demonstrate hours with a 95% confidence if no failure occur during the test . We will assume a Weibull distribution with a shape parameter .

results.content:
Finally, we recommend a risk-assessment methodology for selecting acceptable reliability and statistical confidence levels that may be used in the two RDT methods highlighted. ... This method is also known as the classical reliability demonstration test. When x is the number of observed failures out of i trials, and the probability of each trial p is an independent Bernoulli trial, then the probability of at least c failures can be calculated using a binomial distribution (Equation 1).
Therefore, herein we detail one such appropriate statistical approach to calculate sample size: “Reliability demonstration tests” (RDTs) can be used to verify whether a product or process has met a certain reliability requirement at a stated statistical confidence level when the underlying data distribution is unknown. A number of different RDT approaches are available for process engineers to choose from, including cumulative binomial, nonparametric binomial, exponential chi-squared, and nonparametric Bayesian methods.
Based on the RPN, the risk associated with a given attribute or parameter is classified as high, medium, or low (Table 2). For each risk classification, Table 2 also lists recommendations for confidence (C) and reliability (R). The latter is based on rules of thumb for Cronbach’s alpha coefficient, which measures the reliability or internal consistency of a measurement method (1). ... We recommended documenting the statistical methods and rationality for selecting sample sizes to ensure that a CAPA is effective. For determining sample sizes, both nonparametric binomial and exponential chi-squared methods are widely accepted for practice in industries regulated by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA).

Now the number of sample sizes can be determined by equating Equation 3 to a significance value (α) type 1 error — in other words, the probability of falsely rejecting a true null hypothesis (Equation 4). Taking logarithms on both sides in that equation and solving for sample size n, you obtain Equation 5, in which the term C is confidence.

The following example computes the minimum number of units required for a binomial zero-failure test demonstrating 90% reliability at 90% confidence, and the required total test time for a time-censored exponential test with an MTBF target of 1000 hours.
A binomial (attribute) demonstration test treats each unit tested as a pass/fail trial. If n units are tested and all pass (zero failures), the lower confidence bound on reliability is R^n. To demonstrate a target reliability R at confidence level C with zero failures, you need n = ceil(ln(1 − C) / ln(R)) units. For example, to demonstrate R = 0.90 at 90% confidence requires n = ceil(ln(0.1)/ln(0.9)) = 22 units with zero failures.
Required units (binomial): The minimum number of units that must complete the mission without failure. Fewer units makes the test insufficient to demonstrate the reliability claim. Required total time (exponential): The minimum accumulated unit-hours. Distributing this across more units reduces test duration per unit. Higher confidence: Increases the required test burden proportionally. Moving from 90% to 95% confidence noticeably increases sample size or test time.
These tests are known as Reliability Demonstration Tests (RDTs) or Qualification Tests, widely used in aerospace, defense, and medical device programs. The two most common paradigms are the binomial zero-failure test and the time-censored exponential test. ... Reliability test planning links sample size, test duration, confidence level, and target reliability in a single formula.

results.question: What is a binomial reliability demonstration test?
results.answer: A binomial (attribute) demonstration test treats each unit tested as a pass/fail trial. If n units are tested and all pass (zero failures), the lower confidence bound on reliability is R^n. To demonstrate a target reliability R at confidence level C with zero failures, you need n = ceil(ln(1 − C) / ln(R)) units. For example, to demonstrate R = 0.90 at 90% confidence requires n = ceil(ln(0.1)/ln(0.9)) = 22 units with zero failures. Each unit must be tested for the full mission duration or stress equivalent.
results.title: Reliability Test Planning Calculator | MetricGate

results.question: What is a time-censored exponential reliability test?
results.answer: A time-censored exponential test assumes a constant failure rate (exponential lifetime distribution) and tests units for a fixed duration. Under zero failures, the required total accumulated test time is T_total = −MTBF_target × ln(1 − conf). This total time can be distributed across any combination of units and hours per unit: 10 units tested for 100 hours each is equivalent to 100 units tested for 10 hours each. The exponential model is appropriate for electronic components and systems in their useful-life phase where the failure rate is approximately constant.
results.title: Reliability Test Planning Calculator | MetricGate

results.question: What is reliability test planning?
results.answer: Reliability test planning determines the minimum number of units to test (or the minimum total test duration) required to statistically demonstrate that a product meets a specified reliability target at a given confidence level. Zero-failure demonstration tests are the most common approach: if no failures occur during the test, the observed data support a lower confidence bound on reliability that meets or exceeds the target. This is used in aerospace, medical devices, automotive, and electronics industries to validate product reliability before release.
results.title: Reliability Test Planning Calculator | MetricGate

results.question: How do I choose between the binomial and exponential test designs?
results.answer: Use the binomial design when you are testing to success/failure without time-tracking (e.g., each unit either completes the mission or fails), or when the lifetime distribution is unknown and you prefer a distribution-free approach. Use the time-censored exponential design when you track time-to-failure, the failure rate is approximately constant, and you want to demonstrate a mean time between failures (MTBF). The exponential design is more flexible because total test time can be distributed across any number of units.
results.title: Reliability Test Planning Calculator | MetricGate

results.question: How do I run reliability test planning on MetricGate?
results.answer:
Use the Reliability Test Planning calculator on MetricGate by following these steps:

  1. Select the test design: Binomial (no failures) or Time-censored exponential (no failures).
  2. Enter the Target Reliability R (e.g., 0.90) and the Confidence Level (e.g., 0.90).
  3. For the exponential design, also enter the MTBF Target in hours and optionally the Test Time per Unit or Units Available.
  4. Select the Summary and/or What-if grid outputs.
  5. Run the calculator to obtain the required sample size or total test time, plus optional sensitivity analysis.
results.title: Reliability Test Planning Calculator | MetricGate

results.question: What does the what-if grid show?
results.answer: The what-if grid explores how the required sample size (binomial) or total test time (exponential) changes with small variations in the target reliability and confidence level. It presents a table of scenarios ±5% around your input values, helping you evaluate the sensitivity of the test plan to changes in requirements. This is useful for trade-off discussions with stakeholders: a slightly lower reliability target or confidence level may dramatically reduce the number of units or test hours required.

items.question: What is reliability test planning?
items.answer: Reliability test planning determines the minimum number of units to test (or the minimum total test duration) required to statistically demonstrate that a product meets a specified reliability target at a given confidence level. Zero-failure demonstration tests are the most common approach: if no failures occur during the test, the observed data support a lower confidence bound on reliability that meets or exceeds the target. This is used in aerospace, medical devices, automotive, and electronics industries to validate product reliability before release.
items.title: Reliability Test Planning Calculator | MetricGate

items.question: What is a binomial reliability demonstration test?
items.answer: A binomial (attribute) demonstration test treats each unit tested as a pass/fail trial. If n units are tested and all pass (zero failures), the lower confidence bound on reliability is R^n. To demonstrate a target reliability R at confidence level C with zero failures, you need n = ceil(ln(1 − C) / ln(R)) units. For example, to demonstrate R = 0.90 at 90% confidence requires n = ceil(ln(0.1)/ln(0.9)) = 22 units with zero failures. Each unit must be tested for the full mission duration or stress equivalent.
items.title: Reliability Test Planning Calculator | MetricGate

items.question: What is a time-censored exponential reliability test?
items.answer: A time-censored exponential test assumes a constant failure rate (exponential lifetime distribution) and tests units for a fixed duration. Under zero failures, the required total accumulated test time is T_total = −MTBF_target × ln(1 − conf). This total time can be distributed across any combination of units and hours per unit: 10 units tested for 100 hours each is equivalent to 100 units tested for 10 hours each. The exponential model is appropriate for electronic components and systems in their useful-life phase where the failure rate is approximately constant.
items.title: Reliability Test Planning Calculator | MetricGate

items.question: How do I choose between the binomial and exponential test designs?
items.answer: Use the binomial design when you are testing to success/failure without time-tracking (e.g., each unit either completes the mission or fails), or when the lifetime distribution is unknown and you prefer a distribution-free approach. Use the time-censored exponential design when you track time-to-failure, the failure rate is approximately constant, and you want to demonstrate a mean time between failures (MTBF). The exponential design is more flexible because total test time can be distributed across any number of units.
items.title: Reliability Test Planning Calculator | MetricGate

items.question: What does the what-if grid show?
items.answer: The what-if grid explores how the required sample size (binomial) or total test time (exponential) changes with small variations in the target reliability and confidence level. It presents a table of scenarios ±5% around your input values, helping you evaluate the sensitivity of the test plan to changes in requirements. This is useful for trade-off discussions with stakeholders: a slightly lower reliability target or confidence level may dramatically reduce the number of units or test hours required.
items.title: Reliability Test Planning Calculator | MetricGate

items.question: How do I run reliability test planning on MetricGate?
items.answer:
Use the Reliability Test Planning calculator on MetricGate by following these steps:

  1. Select the test design: Binomial (no failures) or Time-censored exponential (no failures).
  2. Enter the Target Reliability R (e.g., 0.90) and the Confidence Level (e.g., 0.90).
  3. For the exponential design, also enter the MTBF Target in hours and optionally the Test Time per Unit or Units Available.
  4. Select the Summary and/or What-if grid outputs.
  5. Run the calculator to obtain the required sample size or total test time, plus optional sensitivity analysis.
items.title: Reliability Test Planning Calculator | MetricGate

extra_snippets: The following example computes the minimum number of units required for a binomial zero-failure test demonstrating 90% reliability at 90% confidence, and the required total test time for a time-censored exponential test with an MTBF target of 1000 hours.

A binomial (attribute) demonstration test treats each unit tested as a pass/fail trial. If n units are tested and all pass (zero failures), the lower confidence bound on reliability is R^n. To demonstrate a target reliability R at confidence level C with zero failures, you need n = ceil(ln(1 − C) / ln(R)) units. For example, to demonstrate R = 0.90 at 90% confidence requires n = ceil(ln(0.1)/ln(0.9)) = 22 units with zero failures.

Required units (binomial): The minimum number of units that must complete the mission without failure. Fewer units makes the test insufficient to demonstrate the reliability claim. Required total time (exponential): The minimum accumulated unit-hours. Distributing this across more units reduces test duration per unit. Higher confidence: Increases the required test burden proportionally. Moving from 90% to 95% confidence noticeably increases sample size or test time.

These tests are known as Reliability Demonstration Tests (RDTs) or Qualification Tests, widely used in aerospace, defense, and medical device programs. The two most common paradigms are the binomial zero-failure test and the time-censored exponential test. ... Reliability test planning links sample size, test duration, confidence level, and target reliability in a single formula.

extra_snippets: The author of the plan makes reference to a 90/95 confidence and reliability level to determine sample size for attribute type data based on a binomial probability distribution. I always thought that only applied to variables data. I'm typically used to seeing LTPD or AQL levels to characterize attribute data. Is there is a correlation to the 90/95 C and R? Thanks! ... Re: Confidence and Reliability This is probably a reliability demonstration test plan. Units are tested to a specific point in time with no more than a specified number of failures to demonstrate a specified reliability (R) at a specified confidence (.95 or .90) level.

I have seen a table but can't locate it in any textbooks that I have. The table I am referring to is based on the Bayes Success Run Theorem for reliability and confidence, e.g. reliability of 95% and a confidence of 95% with 0 failures allowed in the samples would require 59 samples.

I use Minitab, which can determine the appropriate sample size for a demonstration test. Unfortunately, they do not have this information available online. extra_snippets: In this article, we will introduce the cumulative binomial equation and explore two potential applications for reliability engineering. First, we will explain how the equation can be applied for designing a Reliability Demonstration Test (RDT) that will be effective for demonstrating that a certain product has met or exceeded a given reliability at a given confidence interval.

When prompted (as shown in Figure 1), enter a β value of 1.8 and a 90% confidence level, and click OK. The value of η is automatically calculated, as shown in Figure 2. Figure 1: Enter the value of beta and the confidence level. Figure 2: Calculated value of eta. Case 2: Assume that you have field data from four products and the observation period ended at a different time for each one. Again, no failures were observed.

extra_snippets: This section presents an example of how to use the Reliability Demonstration Test (RDT) tool to calculate the test time needed to show a specified reliability at a specified confidence level in a zero-failure demonstration test. A parametric binomial table will also be created which displays a range of test duration values as a function of sample size and number of allowable failures · A reliability engineer is asked to plan a test for a component whose failure behavior is governed by a Weibull distribution, where beta = 1.5. The goal of the test is to show, with 90% confidence, that the component has a reliability of at least 90% at 100 hours.

In other words, if 10 units are tested for 168.4064 hours at normal use conditions and none fail, then the test will demonstrate, with 90% confidence, that the component has a reliability of at least 90% at 100 hours. To generate a parametric binomial table, click the Create Table of Results icon. In the Solve for area, select Test time for given sample size so the table shows test duration values as a function of sample and number of allowable failures.

Due to resource constraints, it is decided that the target metric will be demonstrated in a zero-failure test. The engineer’s task is to determine the minimum test time needed to demonstrate this reliability in such a test. Follow the steps outlined below to determine the required test time. Open the tool by choosing Insert > Tools > Test Design. In the Test Design Assistant, select Reliability Demonstration Test Design and click OK. Select Parametric Binomial from the Test Design Method area of the control panel.
